era_context: The late 17th century: the Qing Dynasty under Kangxi consolidates power in China; the Ottoman Empire faces increasing pressure from European powers; Louis XIV's France dominates Western Europe; English politics are turbulent under Charles II; European colonial expansion accelerates in North America, Asia, and Africa.

events.title: Peter the Great becomes Tsar of Russia
events.description: Peter Alexeyevich (Peter the Great) becomes Tsar of Russia following the death of his half-brother Feodor III. Peter is initially a joint ruler with his half-brother Ivan V, with their sister Sophia serving as regent. Peter is only 10 years old at his accession, and real power is held by Sophia and her advisor Vasily Golitsyn. Peter's reign will eventually transform Russia into a major European power, but in 1682 he is a minor with limited actual authority. The succession is marked by the Streltsy Revolt, a mutiny of the palace guard that results in significant bloodshed and strengthens Sophia's position.

sources.name: Massie, Peter the Great: His Life and World

events.certainty_note: Peter became Tsar on 27 April 1682 following Feodor III's death; extensively documented in Russian state papers and contemporary accounts.

events.title: Louis XIV moves court to Versailles
events.description: King Louis XIV of France officially moves the royal court from Paris to the Palace of Versailles, which he has been constructing since 1661. The move consolidates royal power by removing the court from Paris, where it had been subject to urban unrest and the influence of the Parlement. Versailles becomes the center of French political power and a symbol of absolute monarchy. The move also serves to control the French nobility by requiring them to reside at court, where they can be monitored and their power curtailed. The palace becomes the model for royal residences across Europe.

sources.name: French court records and royal decrees

events.certainty_note: Louis XIV moved the court to Versailles in May 1682; extensively documented in French state papers and contemporary accounts.

events.title: La Salle reaches the Gulf of Mexico
events.description: French explorer René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle, completes his descent of the Mississippi River and reaches the Gulf of Mexico in April 1682. He claims the entire Mississippi River valley for France, naming it 'Louisiana' in honor of King Louis XIV. This expedition establishes French territorial claims in North America and sets the stage for French colonial expansion in the interior of the continent. La Salle's expedition is one of the most significant European explorations of North America in the 17th century.
key_figures: René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle (French explorer)

sources.name: La Salle's expedition journals and reports to French authorities

events.certainty_note: La Salle reached the Gulf of Mexico on 9 April 1682; extensively documented in French colonial records and contemporary accounts.
cross_references: 1684-001 (La Salle's later attempt to establish a colony at the Gulf)

events.title: Kangxi Emperor consolidates power in Qing China
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor, who ascended to the throne in 1661 as a minor, consolidates his personal power by removing the regents who had governed in his name. The Kangxi Emperor eliminates the influence of the Oboi regency and establishes direct imperial rule. This consolidation of power allows him to pursue his agenda of territorial expansion and administrative reform. The year 1682 marks a turning point in his reign, as he moves from being a figurehead to an active ruler.
key_figures: Oboi (former regent, removed from power earlier)
events.certainty_note: The Kangxi Emperor's consolidation of power was a gradual process; 1682 marks a significant point in this process, though the exact dating is somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Halley's Comet observed and recorded
events.description: Halley's Comet appears in the night sky in 1682, observed by astronomers across Europe and Asia. The comet is recorded by Edmond Halley and other European astronomers, as well as by Chinese and Islamic astronomers. Halley's observations of the comet lead him to calculate its orbital period and predict its return in 1758, establishing the comet as a periodic phenomenon. This observation contributes to the development of celestial mechanics and the understanding of the solar system.
events.certainty_note: Halley's Comet appeared in 1682; extensively documented in astronomical records from multiple civilizations.
cross_references: 1687-001 (Newton's Principia, which provides theoretical framework for Halley's calculations)

events.title: Asante Kingdom consolidates power in West Africa
events.description: The Asante Kingdom, under the leadership of Osei Tutu and his advisor Okomfo Anokye, consolidates power in the region of present-day Ghana. The Asante Confederacy is formed through the unification of several Akan states, with the Golden Stool serving as a symbol of unity and legitimacy. This consolidation establishes the Asante as a major power in West Africa and sets the stage for their expansion and dominance in the region over the following centuries. The Asante Kingdom becomes one of the most powerful African states of the 18th century.

sources.name: Oral traditions and later compilations of Asante history
European trading post records (limited contemporary documentation)

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of the Asante Kingdom occurred in the late 17th century; the exact year is uncertain, but 1682 is within the period of consolidation. Dating relies heavily on oral traditions and later compilations.

disconfirming_evidence: The portrayal of Louis XIV's move to Versailles (1682-002) as a straightforward consolidation of absolute power is complicated by modern scholarship, which notes that the nobility retained significant influence and that the move was partly motivated by practical considerations (Paris was overcrowded and unhealthy). Peter the Great's accession (1682-001) is sometimes presented as the beginning of his transformative reign, but in reality, he had minimal power in 1682 and did not assume full control until 1689. The significance of La Salle's Gulf expedition (1682-003) is sometimes overstated; while it established French territorial claims, these claims were not effectively colonized until much later, and the expedition did not immediately lead to permanent French settlement in Louisiana. The dating of the Asante consolidation (1682-006) is highly uncertain and relies on oral traditions that may have been shaped by later political needs.
historiographic_note: The year 1682 is well-documented in European sources, particularly for France, Russia, and England. Chinese sources are also detailed. However, the historical record remains heavily weighted toward elite political and military events. The record for sub-Saharan Africa relies heavily on oral traditions and later European accounts, which may distort or omit indigenous perspectives. The record for the Pacific and Central Asia remains sparse. European sources on North American exploration reflect European perspectives and may downplay or omit indigenous responses to European presence. The scientific observation of Halley's Comet is well-documented across multiple civilizations, but the significance attributed to it varies by source tradition.

graph_edges.note: Peter's accession in 1682 as a minor set the stage for his later assumption of full power in 1689.
The move to Versailles in 1682 established the palace as the center of French power for the remainder of Louis XIV's reign.
La Salle's successful descent of the Mississippi prompted his later attempt to establish a colony at the Gulf.
Halley's observations of the comet in 1682 contributed to the development of celestial mechanics, which Newton formalized in the Principia (1687).
The consolidation of the Asante Kingdom in 1682 set the stage for their expansion and victory over Denkyira in 1701.
